name: RQ (Redis Queue — Simple Python Job Queue)

auth.notes: Redis connection auth via Redis URL

performance.notes: Redis-backed; sync workers; simple and reliable

agent_readiness.notes: Simple Redis-backed job queue for Python. q = Queue(connection=Redis()); q.enqueue(func, args). Worker: rq worker. RQ Dashboard for monitoring (rq-dashboard package). Job results, retries, TTL. Multiple queues with priorities. Failed job registry. Scheduler via rq-scheduler. Much simpler than Celery — no broker config, no serialization config, no AMQP. Enqueue any Python function. Sync (not async). RQ Exporter for Prometheus. Widely used in Flask applications.

gotchas: Sync workers only (use arq for async Python)
No built-in complex workflow support (use Celery chains or Temporal)
rq-dashboard not maintained as actively as Celery Flower
best_when: Flask/Django apps needing simple background job queue
Teams wanting minimal config compared to Celery
Redis already in stack and sync Python codebase
Simple job queues without complex workflow requirements
avoid_when: Complex task workflows with dependencies (use Celery or Temporal)
summary: RQ (Redis Queue) is the simplest Python job queue — enqueue any function with q.enqueue(func, args). Redis-backed, zero config, sync workers. rq-dashboard for monitoring. Much simpler than Celery for basic background jobs. Best for Flask/Django apps where simplicity matters over features. Use arq for async Python, Celery for AMQP/complex workflows, or Temporal/Prefect for durable execution DAGs.
